did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses supervised machine learning algorithms (specifically XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, and SVM) to classify melanoma skin cancer. It employs hybrid texture feature extraction (ABCD, GLCM, LBP, Histogram of Gradients) and Otsu thresholding for segmentation on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive and PH2 dataset to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed machine learning-based melanoma detection algorithm using hybrid texture features (ABCD, GLCM, LBP, HG) and supervised classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, SVM). It details a specific methodology including image acquisition from ISIC and PH2 datasets, segmentation via Otsu thresholding, feature extraction, and classification, followed by simulation results and performance evaluation (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) compared to existing works. Although published in a conference proceedings (CONIT), it reports new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Machine Learning Based Melanoma Skin Cancer using Hybrid Texture Features
brief_summary: This study proposes a machine learning-based system for detecting and classifying melanoma skin cancer using hybrid texture features. The authors utilized a combined dataset from the ISIC archive and the PH2 dermoscopic images, totaling 10,798 images, which were segmented using Otsu thresholding. Hybrid features including ABCD parameters, GLCM, LBP, and Histogram of Gradients were extracted from the segmented lesions. These features were trained using four supervised classifiers: XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, and SVM, evaluated via 10-fold cross-validation. The results demonstrated that the XGBoost classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 95.38%, outperforming the other classifiers and existing literature methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the classification task. The paper focuses on detecting and classifying melanoma skin cancer. The dataset includes 'malignant' lesions identified as melanoma from the ISIC archive and PH2 dataset.

Used as the negative class for binary classification against melanoma. The dataset includes 'benign' lesions from the ISIC archive and PH2 dataset. The paper notes that distinguishing between benign and malignant is difficult visually.
Mentioned in the Introduction and Literature Survey as a type of skin cancer, but not included in the experimental dataset or classification task of this specific study.
Mentioned in the Introduction and Literature Survey as a type of skin cancer, but not included in the experimental dataset or classification task of this specific study.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Malignant Melanoma vs. Benign Skin Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions using hybrid texture features (ABCD, GLCM, LBP, HG) and supervised classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, SVM). The output is predictive labels for these discrete classes, evaluated via accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision on a 2x2 confusion matrix.

datasets_used.name: Combined ISIC and PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset compiled from the ISIC archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) and the PH2 dermoscopic images database. The study also references SkinVision BV for normal and melanoma images in the bibliography, but explicitly states the database was created by compiling photos from ISIC and PH2.

use.training: Used for training the supervised classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, SVM) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.other: The dataset was segmented using Otsu thresholding and used for hybrid texture feature extraction (ABCD, GLCM, LBP, HG).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to train and test the classifiers (LightGBM, Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM) on the combined dataset to evaluate performance metrics.
Used to calculate and report accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and precision for the binary classification of melanoma vs. benign lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and precision derived from confusion matrices, and compared qualitatively to existing literature.

Combined ISIC and PH2 Dataset.notes: XGBoost achieved the highest accuracy (95.38%) among the classifiers. The results were compared with existing works in literature (Ref [17], [18], [19]), claiming superiority in accuracy due to rich feature selection.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Texture Feature Extraction with Supervised Classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, SVM)

components: ABCD parameters (Asymmetry, Border Irregularity, Color Score, Diameter)
GLCM (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix) features (Energy, Contrast, Correlation, Homogeneity)
feature_extraction.segmentation: Otsu Thresholding with flood filling and morphology transformation (erosion/dilation) to remove hairs

classifiers.name: XGBoost
classifiers.description: Gradient-boosting framework, highly efficient and portable

classifiers.name: LightGBM
classifiers.description: Gradient-boosting framework using tree-based learning algorithm

classifiers.name: Random Forest
classifiers.description: Ensemble method applying several classifiers on subsets of datasets

hyperparameters.n_estimators: Not explicitly reported (referred to as 'n estimator')

training_details.implementation: OpenCV python for feature extraction, Scikit-learn for machine learning algorithms

preprocessing_techniques: Flood filling to fill the segmented contour
Morphology transformation (erosion and dilation) to remove hairs from the segmented area
conclusions: The proposed machine learning-based system using hybrid texture features (ABCD, GLCM, LBP, and Histogram of Gradients) effectively detects and classifies melanoma skin cancer.

Among the tested supervised classifiers (SVM, LightGBM, Random Forest, and XGBoost), XGBoost achieved the highest accuracy of 95.38%, outperforming the other classifiers and existing literature methods.
The rich feature selection of hybrid texture features contributes to the superior classification accuracy of the proposed algorithm.
The automated skin detection system saves the time of dermatologists and patients, facilitating early-stage treatment preparation.
important_remarks: The study combines the ISIC archive and PH2 datasets with external images from SkinVision BV (references [14]-[16]), creating a heterogeneous dataset that may introduce domain shift or bias not accounted for in the evaluation.

The paper claims superiority over existing literature (Refs [17], [18], [19]) based on accuracy comparisons, but does not provide statistical significance testing (p-values) or confidence intervals to support these claims.

The dataset split is explicitly defined as 7018 training, 780 validation, and 3000 testing images, yet the methodology states '10-fold cross-validation is used where all samples were trained and tested,' which is methodologically ambiguous regarding whether the test set was held out during CV or if the CV was performed on the training/validation split only.

The SVM classifier performed poorly (Accuracy: 66.48%) compared to tree-based ensemble methods (XGBoost: 95.38%), suggesting the hybrid texture features may be better suited for gradient boosting algorithms than linear or kernel-based SVMs in this context.

The feature extraction relies on manual segmentation via Otsu thresholding and morphological operations to remove hairs; errors in this preprocessing step (e.g., incomplete hair removal or over-segmentation) could significantly impact feature quality and downstream classification performance.

The ABCD feature calculation uses a fixed asymmetry threshold of 0.999 to determine malignancy, which is a rigid heuristic that may not generalize well across diverse lesion morphologies compared to learned features.

The paper does not report class imbalance metrics or use techniques to address potential imbalance between benign and malignant classes, despite the known prevalence differences in skin cancer datasets.

No external validation on a completely independent dataset was performed; the model was only evaluated on the combined ISIC/PH2/SkinVision dataset, limiting the generalizability of the results to other clinical populations or imaging devices.
